name: Cloak of Heavenly Feathers
description: The Hagoromo, or Cloak of Heavenly Feathers, is a legendary garment in Japanese folklore, most famously associated with the tale of the Tennin (celestial maiden). This ethereal cloak is not merely clothing; it is a symbol of divinity, grace, and the connection between the mortal and celestial realms. Crafted from the plumage of mythical birds or woven from moonlight itself, the Hagoromo grants its wearer the ability to fly and ascend to the heavens. Its feathers shimmer with otherworldly light, and its touch bestows upon the wearer a sense of profound peace and tranquility. The cloak's significance lies in its representation of the delicate balance between the human and divine, and the consequences of disrupting that harmony. Losing the Hagoromo, as depicted in the Tennin legend, signifies a loss of one's connection to the divine and a grounding in the mortal world. The Hagoromo is often depicted in Noh theater and other traditional arts, serving as a powerful visual representation of celestial beauty and the yearning for transcendence. It represents the ultimate freedom and the power to transcend earthly limitations. The cloak's ethereal nature also makes it vulnerable to theft or loss, highlighting the fragility of the connection between heaven and earth.
subtitle: Celestial garment granting flight and divinity
origin: Crafted by celestial beings, bestowed upon Tennin.

keyMyths.title: The Tennin's Robe
keyMyths.description: A fisherman steals the Hagoromo of a Tennin bathing on the beach. She cannot return to heaven without it, and he eventually returns it after she dances for him.

keyMyths.title: Flight to the Heavens
keyMyths.description: The Hagoromo grants the wearer the ability to fly and ascend to the heavenly realm. It is the only means for a Tennin to return to their celestial home.

sources.author: Donald Philippi
sources.work: Songs of Gods, Songs of Humans: The Epic Tradition of the Ainu
sources.description: Provides context for Japanese mythology and folklore.
sources.text: Provides context for Japanese mythology and folklore.
